name: org.machanism.machai:genai-client
description: GenAI Client is a Java library in the MachAI ecosystem for integrating JVM applications with generative AI providers through a unified provider abstraction. It offers provider-neutral chat and embedding access for enterprise applications that need prompt orchestration, semantic search, tool-enabled AI workflows, and runtime-selectable backends. The central contract is org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai, which supports provider initialization with a model and configuration, adding prompts, setting instructions, registering tools, executing requests, clearing session state, logging inputs, setting a working directory, and reading usage metrics. Provider creation is centralized in org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager, which resolves conversational and embedding providers from model identifiers. Built-in implementations include org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.openai.OpenAIProvider, which adapts the OpenAI Java SDK Responses API and supports prompting, iterative tool calling, optional built-in web search, MCP server registration, usage capture, and embedding generation; org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.anthropic.AnthropicProvider, which adapts the Anthropic Java SDK for Claude-based prompting with configurable base URL, timeout, output token limits, and MCP plus web-search registration; and org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.codemie.CodeMieProvider, which authenticates against an OIDC token endpoint, configures a CodeMie OpenAI-compatible backend, and delegates to downstream providers based on model families such as gpt-* and claude-*. Common provider behavior is factored into org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.AbstractAIProvider, which centralizes configuration handling, request input logging, safe tool invocation, timeout handling, usage accounting, and bootstrap of MCP and web-search capabilities. The library also includes org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.GenaiAdapter for provider decoration and delegation, org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider for vector generation, org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.ToolFunction as the callback interface for executable tools, org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools as an SPI for reusable tool bundles, and org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader for ServiceLoader-based discovery and application of those bundles. Configuration keys documented by the Javadocs include OPENAI_API_KEY, OPENAI_BASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL, GENAI_TIMEOUT, MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS, MAX_TOOL_CALLS, WebSearchTool.type, WebSearchTool.city, WebSearchTool.country, WebSearchTool.region, MCP.url, MCP.label, MCP.description, MCP.authorization, GENAI_USERNAME, GENAI_PASSWORD, AUTH_URL, LOG_INPUTS_PROP_NAME, and SERVERID_PROP_NAME. This artifact is suitable for AI-assisted automation, prompt-driven business workflows, semantic retrieval, agent-style tool execution, external-context enrichment, and reusable host-side capability packaging in Java applications.

usageContext: JVM applications that require a provider-neutral API for prompt execution and response generation
Systems that switch between OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, and EPAM CodeMie backends by configuration rather than code changes
Applications that expose host-side Java functions to LLMs through tool-calling workflows
Projects using ServiceLoader-discovered tool bundles for modular AI capability installation
Semantic-search and retrieval-augmented workflows that need embedding generation and similarity search
Enterprise automation scenarios that combine prompts, tools, MCP servers, and provider-native web search
Libraries and applications that need configurable timeouts, token limits, input logging, and usage tracking for AI requests

constructors.description: Creates a conversational provider instance from a provider-qualified model string and a Configurator. This is the primary provider-neutral bootstrap entry point for applications that want to select OpenAI, Claude, or CodeMie at runtime.

examples.description: How to use it: add the Maven dependency for org.machanism.machai:genai-client:1.1.15, configure the provider credentials and timeout, and then resolve the provider with a Provider:Model identifier. This is the standard bootstrap path for provider-neutral AI integration.
How to use it with CodeMie: configure CodeMie credentials, resolve a CodeMie-prefixed model, and continue using the returned Genai instance through the same neutral API used by other providers.

constructors.description: Constructs a loader that discovers org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools implementations using java.util.ServiceLoader. Use it when an application wants to install reusable host-side tool bundles without manually wiring each tool.

examples.snippet: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader loader = new org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader();
examples.description: How to use it: instantiate the loader during application startup after adding the dependency. Then call applyTools on a configured provider to register all discovered tool installers.

constructors.description: Creates a delegating Genai adapter without an initial delegate. It is intended for decoration and cross-cutting behavior, such as logging, retries, metrics, or request shaping, while preserving the Genai contract.

examples.snippet:
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.GenaiAdapter adapter = new org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.GenaiAdapter();
adapter.setProvider(org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("OpenAI:gpt-4o-mini", new org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator()));
examples.description: How to use it: create the adapter, assign a delegate provider with setProvider, and then invoke the standard Genai methods through the adapter. This is useful when you want to wrap an existing provider instance with extra host-side behavior.

features.name: prompt
features.description: Adds a user prompt to the current provider session. This is the standard method for accumulating request content before invoking perform().

examples.description: Practical how-to: install the Maven dependency, configure OPENAI_API_KEY, resolve a provider, define instructions, add a prompt, and execute the request with perform(). This is the shortest working path for a chat-style request.

features.name: instructions
features.description: Sets system or session instructions that shape response style and behavior. Use it for role definition, output constraints, formatting guidance, or behavioral rules before prompts are sent.

examples.description: Practical how-to: install the dependency, configure the Anthropic key, create a Claude-backed provider, define stable instructions, and execute the prompt. This pattern is useful when the application needs predictable response style across many requests.

features.name: perform
features.description: Executes the current request using the configured provider, accumulated prompts, instructions, optional tools, and provider-specific capabilities. For OpenAI-backed execution, tool calls can be resolved iteratively until final text is produced.

examples.description: Practical how-to: after installing the dependency and configuring credentials, prepare the request state with prompt data and call perform() once. In tool-enabled flows, the provider can internally execute tool rounds before returning the final answer.

features.name: addTool
features.signature: void org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.openai.OpenAIProvider.addTool(java.lang.String name, java.lang.String description, org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.ToolFunction function, java.lang.String... paramsDesc)
features.description: Registers a function tool on an OpenAI-backed provider. Parameter descriptors follow the format name:type:required:description and are converted to a JSON Schema object that the model can use for tool invocation.

examples.description: Step-by-step how to use it: 1) add org.machanism.machai:genai-client to pom.xml, 2) configure OPENAI_API_KEY, 3) create the provider, 4) set a working directory if tool execution needs file context, 5) register the tool with parameter descriptors, 6) prompt the model to use it, and 7) call perform() to complete the workflow.

features.name: applyTools
features.description: Discovers FunctionTools implementations, creates fresh instances, optionally injects a Configurator, and applies their tool registrations to the specified provider. This enables modular packaging of tool bundles for AI-capable hosts.

examples.description: Step-by-step how to use it: 1) install the library dependency, 2) place FunctionTools implementations and META-INF/services registration on the classpath, 3) create the provider, 4) call applyTools, and 5) execute prompts that can benefit from the installed tools.

features.name: getEmbeddingProvider
features.description: Resolves an embedding-capable provider from a model identifier and configuration. This is the provider-neutral entry point for vector generation, semantic indexing, similarity search, and retrieval-augmented workflows.

examples.description: Practical how-to: add the Maven dependency, configure provider credentials, resolve an embedding provider through GenaiProviderManager, and call embedding() for indexing, ranking, semantic retrieval, or RAG scenarios.

features.name: embedding
features.description: Requests an embedding vector for the supplied text from an OpenAI-compatible embedding model. The returned vector can be used for semantic indexing, nearest-neighbor search, clustering, and ranking.

examples.description: How to use it: add the dependency, configure OPENAI_API_KEY, resolve an embedding model, and generate vectors from business text that can later be stored in a vector database or compared in semantic search workflows.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai
customizations.description: Primary extension contract for provider integrations. Custom implementations can supply initialization, prompt accumulation, tool registration, execution, input logging, usage reporting, and working-directory handling behind a stable API.

examples.description: How to use this customization point: implement a provider for a new backend, package it in an integration module, and keep the rest of the system coded against the Genai abstraction rather than a concrete provider class.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools
customizations.description: SPI for reusable host-side tool bundles. Implementations can register one or more tools, optionally consume Configurator values, and be discovered at runtime through ServiceLoader.

examples.description: How to use this customization point: create a FunctionTools implementation, register it in META-INF/services/org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools, add the module to the classpath, and let FunctionToolsLoader discover and apply it automatically.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.GenaiAdapter
customizations.description: A decoration and delegation extension point for wrapping one Genai provider with another layer that forwards calls while adding host-side behavior such as logging, retries, metrics, request shaping, or policy checks.

examples.description: How to use this customization point: subclass GenaiAdapter, override selected methods, assign a delegate provider through setProvider, and expose the wrapped instance to application code when cross-cutting behavior is needed.

customizations.name: Provider configuration for web search and MCP
customizations.description: Providers based on the documented configuration model can be customized using properties for timeouts, token limits, web search, input logging, server targeting, and MCP server registration. Relevant keys include GENAI_TIMEOUT, MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS, MAX_TOOL_CALLS, WebSearchTool.type, WebSearchTool.city, WebSearchTool.country, WebSearchTool.region, MCP.url, MCP.label, MCP.description, MCP.authorization, LOG_INPUTS_PROP_NAME, SERVERID_PROP_NAME, and numbered MCP groups.

examples.description: How to use this customization point: install the dependency, populate the relevant configuration keys, create the provider, and let the provider attach built-in web search and MCP capabilities without additional Java wiring.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai
studs.description: Core abstraction for provider-neutral conversational AI workflows, including initialization, prompting, instruction management, tool registration, execution, state clearing, input logging, working-directory propagation, and usage retrieval.

examples.description: How to use it: code the application against the Genai interface instead of a concrete provider so backend selection stays configuration-driven and provider-neutral.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider
studs.description: Abstraction for providers that can compute embedding vectors from text. It is intended for semantic search, similarity comparison, ranking, clustering, and retrieval-augmented workflows.

examples.description: How to use it: resolve an embedding-capable provider separately from a conversational provider when the application only needs vector generation for indexing or retrieval.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools
studs.description: SPI contract for modules that contribute one or more reusable host-side function tools to a compatible provider instance.

examples.description: How to use it: implement the interface, contribute tool registrations in applyTools, and package the implementation for ServiceLoader-based discovery.

examples.title: Install the library as a Maven dependency and execute a basic prompt
steps: Add the Maven dependency for org.machanism.machai:genai-client:1.1.15 to your pom.xml.
Make sure your application runs on Java 8 or newer.
Set the OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable or provide the same value through org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator.
Create a Configurator and resolve a provider with org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("OpenAI:gpt-4.1", conf).
Call instructions() and prompt() to prepare the request.
Call perform() and consume the returned answer.
examples.snippet:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.machanism.machai</groupId>
  <artifactId>genai-client</artifactId>
  <version>1.1.15</version>
</dependency>

org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf = new org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator();
conf.set("OPENAI_API_KEY", java.lang.System.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"));
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider = org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("OpenAI:gpt-4.1", conf);
provider.instructions("You are a helpful Java assistant.");
provider.prompt("Explain what a provider abstraction solves in one paragraph.");
java.lang.String answer = provider.perform();
examples.description: A practical how-to scenario for first-time adoption. It covers installation, credential setup, provider creation, prompt submission, and request execution with the minimum necessary steps.

examples.title: Configure and use CodeMie as the provider backend
steps: Add the genai-client Maven dependency to your application.
Provide GENAI_USERNAME and GENAI_PASSWORD through environment variables or Configurator values.
Optionally set AUTH_URL if your organization uses a non-default CodeMie token endpoint.
Resolve the provider with a CodeMie-prefixed model such as CodeMie:gpt-4o-mini or CodeMie:claude-3-5-sonnet.
Call prompt() and perform() exactly as you would for other providers because the API remains provider-neutral.
examples.snippet:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.machanism.machai</groupId>
  <artifactId>genai-client</artifactId>
  <version>1.1.15</version>
</dependency>

org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf = new org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator();
conf.set(org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai.USERNAME_PROP_NAME, java.lang.System.getenv(org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai.USERNAME_PROP_NAME));
conf.set(org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai.PASSWORD_PROP_NAME, java.lang.System.getenv(org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai.PASSWORD_PROP_NAME));
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider = org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("CodeMie:gpt-4o-mini", conf);
provider.prompt("Create a short test plan for a REST API client.");
java.lang.String answer = provider.perform();
examples.description: A real-world how-to for organizations that authenticate through EPAM CodeMie and want to keep application code unchanged while switching model families via configuration.

examples.title: Install, configure, and run functional tools through ServiceLoader
steps: Add the genai-client dependency to your Maven build so provider and tool SPI APIs are available.
Create a FunctionTools implementation in your application or a companion library.
Register that implementation in META-INF/services/org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools.
Configure the provider with the required API key and create it through GenaiProviderManager.
Construct FunctionToolsLoader and call applyTools(provider, conf).
Prompt the model with a task that can benefit from the installed tools, then call perform().
examples.snippet:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.machanism.machai</groupId>
  <artifactId>genai-client</artifactId>
  <version>1.1.15</version>
</dependency>

public final class com.example.tools.TimeFunctionTools implements org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools {
  public void applyTools(org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider) {
    provider.addTool("current_time", "Returns the current UTC timestamp", (com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.JsonNode params, java.io.File projectDir) -> java.time.Instant.now().toString());
  }
}

org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf = new org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator();
conf.set("OPENAI_API_KEY", java.lang.System.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"));
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider = org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("OpenAI:gpt-4.1", conf);
provider.setWorkingDir(new java.io.File("."));
org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader loader = new org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader();
loader.applyTools(provider, conf);
provider.instructions("Use the current_time tool when the user asks for time.");
provider.prompt("What time is it according to the current_time tool?");
java.lang.String answer = provider.perform();
examples.description: A step-by-step how-to that demonstrates installation, tool packaging, ServiceLoader registration, provider setup, tool application, and execution of a real tool-calling workflow.

examples.title: Enable built-in web search and MCP server integration through configuration
steps: Add the Maven dependency for genai-client.
Set OPENAI_API_KEY in the environment or Configurator.
Configure WebSearchTool.type and optional location properties to enable provider-native web search.
Configure MCP.url, MCP.label, and optional MCP.description and MCP.authorization values to attach a remote MCP server.
Create the provider through GenaiProviderManager and execute a prompt that may require external context or remote tools.
examples.description: A configuration-first how-to scenario that shows how to enable external retrieval and MCP-based capabilities without implementing custom Java tools.
